text:
Population Trajectories
Each plot shows estimated adult abundance (blue line) with 50% CI (dark ribbon) and 90% CI (light ribbon) derived from the delta method on the ADREPORT-ed log-scale state. Open circles are observed redd counts scaled to adult-equivalents (redds ÷ 0.5 × survey coverage fraction), placing observations and model estimates on the same axis. The dashed red line marks the 20-adult management threshold.

title: Core Area Status
text:
Core-Area Summary Table







Occupancy = % of local populations with estimated N ≥ 20 adults (last data year). Portfolio Balance = effective number of populations / total populations, derived from the Shannon diversity of the within-core abundance distribution (1 = perfectly balanced; values near 0 indicate dominance by a single population). Qext (total) = P(total core N < 20 × n_pops at any projection year). Qext (majority) = P(majority of populations fall below 20 adults at any projection year). title: Status and Conservation Risk of Threatened Bull Trout
text:
Bull trout (Salvelinus confluentus) are a cold-water obligate salmonid listed as Threatened under the U.S. Endangered Species Act. Across the Columbia Headwaters and Saint Mary Recovery Units, the only populations east of the Continental Divide, many local populations have declined in recent decades due to a combination of habitat degradation, warming and altered stream hydrology, and the spread of non-native fishes.
Despite decades of monitoring effort, our ability to rigorously assess population status and conservation risk has been limited by a lack of data-driven, quantitative approaches operating at scales relevant to management. Single-population trend analyses often have low statistical power, can be sensitive to the time window of available data, and fail to capture the hierarchical structure of bull trout populations, from local spawning streams, to core area metapopulations, to the recovery unit as a whole.
This project was conceived to address that gap. In 2021, a team from the USGS Northern Rocky Mountain Science Center (NOROCK) and the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service was awarded funding through the USGS-FWS Science Support Partnership to develop a unified, spatiotemporal status andthreat assessment for bull trout in these two recovery units. This website presents the products of that effort.

title: Status and Conservation Risk of Threatened Bull Trout
text:
What We Did
The project was organized around three objectives, each corresponding to a section of this website.






1 — Centralized Database



We assembled and synthesized redd count monitoring data from 80 local bull trout populations across 26 core areas, spanning 1984–2022. Environmental covariates (stream temperature, summer base-flow, invasive fish proportion, and lake trout abundance) were compiled from state, federal, and tribal partners and linked to each population at annual resolution.
→ Explore the data








2 — Hierarchical Multi-Population Viability Analysis



We developed a hierarchical state-space Ricker model that integrates all available abundance data across local populations and core areas simultaneously. This approach borrows strength across populations to estimate demographic rates for data-sparse populations, propagates observation and process uncertainty through the analysis, and directly estimates the effects of abiotic and biotic threats on population growth rates (λ) at the local population and core area scales.
→ View model results








3 — Decision-Support Tools



We translated model outputs into an annually-updatable conservation prioritization framework designed to help resource managers identify which populations are most at risk and which management levers (invasive species control, flow augmentation, temperature reduction) offer the greatest potential benefit per population and per core area.
→ Explore the prioritization tools

title: Status and Conservation Risk of Threatened Bull Trout
text:
Species Background
Bull trout require cold (< 13 °C), clean, complex, and connected streams for all life stages. They are highly sensitive to a suite of anthropogenic stressors:







Threat
Mechanism




Warming water temperatures
Upper spawning threshold ~13 °C; lethal above ~20 °C; accelerated warming under climate change


Altered hydrology
Low summer base-flow reduces habitat availability; high winter floods scour incubating eggs


Non-native fish
Competitive displacement by brook trout and brown trout; predation and competition from lake trout in lake-connected systems


Migratory barriers
Loss of access to adfluvial (lake-resident) life history reduces productivity; adfluvial populations support higher λ


Habitat degradation
Road-related fine sediment impairs gravel spawning habitat; riparian degradation increases thermal loading



Bull trout population structure is hierarchical: local spawning populations are nested within core areas (roughly watershed-scale metapopulations), which in turn comprise recovery units. Effective conservation planning must account for dynamics at all three scales. This is a central motivation for the multi-population modeling approach used here.

Model v1_22 · Data v1_10 · Funded by the USGS–FWS Science Support Partnership, FY21-22 Interior Regions 5 and 7

Thermal Performance Curve
Bull trout population growth rate shows a unimodal (hump-shaped) response to August water temperature: growth is fastest at intermediate temperatures and declines at both extremes. The curve is estimated jointly from the linear and quadratic temperature coefficients in the model.

title: Context Prompt: Bull Trout MPVA — Invasive Species Causation Analysis
text:
This is a multi-population viability analysis (MPVA) for bull trout (Salvelinus confluentus) across ~110 local populations in the northern Rocky Mountains (primarily Montana, Idaho, BC). The model is a state-space Ricker growth model implemented in TMB (Template Model Builder) with a three-level spatial hierarchy: global → core area → local population.
The scientific question for this session: we have a strong negative association between invasive species proportion and bull trout population growth rates (bINV_M ≈ −0.5 in full model, z ≈ −3 to −4). However, we are concerned this reflects correlation rather than causation, and we want to strengthen the causal inference or at minimum characterize the direction of influence.
The two competing hypotheses are: - Displacement: invasives cause bull trout decline (INV → ↓BLT). Temporal signature: invasive increase precedes bull trout decline (INV leads BLT). - Replacement: bull trout decline enables invasive expansion (↓BLT → INV). Temporal signature: bull trout decline precedes invasive increase (BLT leads INV). This could occur if climate stress reduces bull trout before invasives arrive, and invasives simply fill the vacated niche.
Both processes can occur simultaneously, and in practice both are likely.




C++ file: Code/MPVA_TMB_BUILDING_1_23.cpp Run script: Code/Run_MPVA_Model1_23.R Functions: Code/MPVA_Functions1_14.R Data prep: Code/PrepareData_MPVA1_8.R


logN(p,t) = logN(p,t-1) + R(p,t) + epsN(p,t-1)

R(p,t) = b0r_pop[p]
        + bFlow_pop[p]  * Flow(p, t-1)     # lagged 6 yr in data matrix
        + bTemp_pop[p]  * Temp(p, t-1)     # lagged 6 yr in data matrix
        + bTemp2        * Temp(p, t-1)^2   # global quadratic
        + bINV_pop[p]   * INV(p, t-1)      # ONE built-in t-1 lag (C++ index)
        + bLKT_M        * LKT(p, t-1)      # lake trout only, global scalar
        - exp(log_b0phi_pop[p]) * N(p,t-1) / extent[p] / 1000   # density dep.




Metric: logit(invasive proportion) z-scored, where invasive proportion = (lake trout

brown trout CPUE) / (all salmonid CPUE) at each patch, each year

Data matrix: INV is nPop × nYears; inv_years_needed = DatYears (no additional lag shift applied in data prep — the t-1 lag is entirely within the C++ loop)
Hierarchy: bINV_pop[p] ~ N(bINV_M, sigma_bINV) — population-level random slopes drawn from a global mean
Current result: bINV_M ≈ −0.5 (negative growth effect of invasives)




PARAMETER(bINV_M);               // global mean invasive effect
PARAMETER_VECTOR(bINV_pop);      // pop-level random slopes [nPop]
PARAMETER(log_sigma_bINV);       // SD of pop-level variation





The INV data is annual (or interpolated to annual) for most populations over roughly 1980–2020. The primary invasive species are lake trout (Salvelinus namaycush) in lake-connected systems and brown trout (Salmo trutta) in stream systems.
Bull trout redd counts (ReddCounts) are the primary abundance index, supplemented by CPUE electrofishing data (CPUEobs) at some populations.
Important: Flow (bFlow_pop) is already lagged 6 years to represent cohort effects through the juvenile stage (spawning flow → juvenile survival → adult recruitment). Temperature similarly. INV currently has only the 1-step (1-year) lag built into the C++ loop.






The 1-year lag (t-1) in the C++ loop may be too short biologically. If invasives reduce bull trout via competition with juveniles, the signal would propagate through the 3–5 year juvenile period before appearing in redd counts. This mirrors the 6-year lag used for flow.
Question: What lag length (0–8 years) produces the best AICc for the INV effect? This would use the same ModelSelection_Lags framework already in the codebase.



To distinguish displacement from replacement, we can test asymmetric cross-lagged effects: - Does INV(t-k) predict BLT residuals (BLT growth unexplained by Ricker model)? → displacement - Does BLT(t-k) predict INV(t) change? → replacement/competitive release
This is most naturally done as a supplementary analysis on model residuals, not within the main TMB model.



Under the climate facilitation hypothesis, invasives are most damaging when temperatures are already stressful for bull trout (near the upper thermal limit). An INV × Temp interaction term would test whether invasive effects are stronger in warm years/populations.
This requires adding an interaction parameter to the C++ model.



Rather than a single lag, we could include INV at multiple lags simultaneously (e.g., t-1 and t-4) to capture both immediate competition effects and longer-term recruitment effects.





Before modifying anything, please read:

Code/MPVA_TMB_BUILDING_1_23.cpp — current C++ model (focus on INV block, ~lines 95–245)
Code/MPVA_Functions1_14.R — R helper functions (init_pars, make_bounds, make_covar_map)
Code/PrepareData_MPVA1_8.R — data preparation (focus on INV block, ~lines 310–345)
Code/ModelSelection_Lags_1_2.R — existing lag model selection framework (to understand how Flow/Temp lags were tested — INV lag test would follow same pattern)
Code/Run_MPVA_Model1_23.R — three-stage fitting script (A → B1 → B2)





Always increment version numbers. Never overwrite existing files.

C++ files: MPVA_TMB_BUILDING_1_XX.cpp (next = 1_24)
Functions: MPVA_Functions1_XX.R (next = 1_15)
Run scripts: Run_MPVA_Model1_XX.R (next = 1_24)
Data prep: PrepareData_MPVA1_X.R (next = 1_9 if data changes needed)
Model selection: ModelSelection_Lags_1_X.R (next = 1_3)














Priority
Analysis
Effort
Insight




1
Lag selection for INV (0–7 yr) using existing lag framework
Low
High — tells us biological timescale


2
Cross-lag regression on model residuals (Granger-style)
Medium
High — direction of causation


3
INV × Temp interaction in C++ model
Medium
Medium — climate facilitation test


4
Multi-lag INV (t-1 + t-k)
High
Medium — may over-parameterise



Recommended first step: Adapt ModelSelection_Lags_1_2.R to test INV lags 0–7 while holding Flow (lag 6) and Temp (lag 6) fixed. This will tell us whether the 1-year lag is biologically appropriate and whether a longer lag improves fit.
Second step: Cross-lag residual analysis — extract log-lambda residuals from the best model, regress them on past INV, and separately regress INV changes on past log-lambda. Compare R² / AIC of INV→BLT vs BLT→INV regressions.





Best static covariate model: Adf + Flood (ΔAICc = 0 vs full model)
Models within ΔAICc 4: Adf+Flood, Adf+VB+Flood, Adf+Flood+RD, Full
Full model static covariate Wald z-scores:

bAdf (adfluvial binary): z = −3.28 ***
bFlood (W95 flood freq): z = −2.35 **
bUVB (valley bottom): z = +1.24 n.s.
bRD (road density): z = −0.85 n.s.

Dynamic covariate summary (full model, approximate):

bINV_M ≈ −0.5 (strong negative, consistent across models)
bTemp_M ≈ −0.3 to −0.4 (negative, quadratic component also negative)
bFlow_M ≈ +0.15 to +0.25 (positive — high flow years boost recruitment)
bLKT_M ≈ −0.2 to −0.3 (lake trout pressure)

Overview
This document presents the model structure and key diagnostics for a hierarchical state-space Ricker model fit to bull trout redd count time series across multiple local populations nested within core areas. Detailed results (e.g., covariate effects, population trajectories, exposure and sensitivity, and future viability projections) are in the Model Results tab.

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Model Description

Process Model
The latent population abundance follows a density-dependent Ricker process with AR(1) process error:
\[\log N_{p,t} = \log N_{p,t-1} + R_{p,t} + \varepsilon_{p,t}\]
where the instantaneous growth rate is:
\[R_{p,t} = b_{0r,p} + \beta_{\text{Flow},p} \cdot \text{Flow}_{p,t-1}
           + \beta_{\text{Temp},p} \cdot T_{p,t-1}
           + \beta_{\text{Temp}_2} \cdot T_{p,t-1}^2
           + \beta_{\text{INV},p} \cdot \text{INV}_{p,t-1}
           + \beta_{\text{LKT},M} \cdot \text{LKT}_{c,t-1}
           - \phi_p \cdot D_{p,t-1}\]
Process errors follow an AR(1) structure:
\[\varepsilon_{p,t} = \rho \cdot \varepsilon_{p,t-1} + \eta_{p,t}, \quad
  \eta_{p,t} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\; \sigma_N \sqrt{1-\rho^2}\right)\]
All dynamic covariates carry an effective lag of 6 years, reflecting the time from larval/juvenile conditions to adult redd counts.


Observation Model
Redd counts at each survey site follow a Negative Binomial (NB2) distribution:
\[C_{s,y} \sim \text{NB2}\!\left(\mu_{s,y},\; \phi_{\text{obs}}\right), \quad
  \mu_{s,y} = 0.5 \cdot N_{p,y} \cdot \pi_s \cdot \exp(\beta_{\text{FC}} \cdot \text{Flow}_{p,y})\]
where \(\phi_{\text{obs}}\) is the NB2 dispersion parameter (\(\text{Var} = \mu + \mu^2/\phi_{\text{obs}}\); as \(\phi_{\text{obs}} \to \infty\) the distribution collapses to Poisson), \(\pi_s\) is the fraction of population habitat surveyed at site \(s\), and \(\beta_{\text{FC}}\) captures the contemporaneous same-year flow effect on redd/adult ratios (observation model only; does not enter the state equation).


CPUE Sub-Model
For populations with electrofishing data, model-based CPUE (fish km⁻¹) provides an independent abundance index:
\[\log(\text{CPUE}_i) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\log N_{p,y} - \log(\text{extent}_p)
  + q_s,\; \sigma_{\text{CPUE}}\right)\]
where \(q_s\) is a section-level catchability random effect that absorbs persistent efficiency differences among electrofishing reaches.


Hierarchical Parameter Structure

Global → Population

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Model Selection

Flow Lag
Bull trout have a complex life history: eggs are deposited in fall, incubate over winter, and juveniles spend several years rearing in streams before migrating to larger rivers or lakes, and eventually returning as adults to spawn. We therefore tested a range of lags between environmental conditions and the observed adult redd count response. With temperature fixed at an effective 6-year lag (the best-supported specification from earlier runs), we searched a grid of effective lags 4–8 years × moving-average windows (none, 2-year, 3-year) for summer streamflow (15 combinations total). All models used the 3-stage NB2 fitting procedure; comparison is by marginal AIC.


Show code
fl <- readRDS(here("Results", "FlowLagSearch_2026-03-22.RDS"))

fl_plot <- fl$results_df |>
  filter(!is.na(AIC)) |>
  mutate(
    ma_label  = case_when(
      MovAvgF == 0 ~ "No moving average",
      MovAvgF == 2 ~ "2-year MA",
      MovAvgF == 3 ~ "3-year MA"
    ),
    label     = factor(label, levels = rev(label[order(AIC)])),
    fill_grp  = case_when(
      delta_AIC <= 2 ~ "Within 2 AIC (equivalent)",
      delta_AIC <= 7 ~ "Within 7 AIC (some support)",
      TRUE           ~ "> 7 AIC (little support)"
    ) |> factor(levels = c("Within 2 AIC (equivalent)",
                            "Within 7 AIC (some support)",
                            "> 7 AIC (little support)"))
  )

ggplot(fl_plot, aes(x = label, y = delta_AIC, fill = fill_grp)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.65) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 2, linetype = 2, color = "goldenrod",  linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 7, linetype = 3, color = "tomato",     linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("Δ%.1f", delta_AIC)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 3.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Within 2 AIC (equivalent)"  = "steelblue",
               "Within 7 AIC (some support)" = "goldenrod3",
               "> 7 AIC (little support)"    = "grey60"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.25))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "ΔAIC relative to best model",
    title    = "Flow lag selection — ΔAIC by lag specification",
    subtitle = sprintf(
      "Temperature fixed: effective %d-yr lag, no smoothing  |  Data v%s  |  Model v%s",
      fl$TempLag + 1, fl$DataVersion, fl$ModelVersion
    ),
    caption  = "NA = model failed to converge; longer lags exceeded the time-series extent"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")









The 6-year effective lag with no moving average (Lag6_NoMA) has the lowest AIC. The next-best models all involve shorter lags with multi-year smoothing, which may be capturing a similar biological signal through averaging. Given the clean biological interpretation of 6 years corresponding to the average age-at-maturity an unsmoothed 6-year lag is preferred. This specification is used in all subsequent model runs.


Static Habitat Covariates
With the flow lag fixed, we tested all 16 subsets of four static habitat scalars that shift the population-level baseline growth rate:

Abbreviation

Lake-connected populations have higher access to productive food sources and thermal refuge in hypolimnetic waters

Often used as a proxy for upwelling potential.

Flood
High-flow winters scour redds and reduce recruitment


Road density in valley bottom (z-score)
RD
Roads increase fine sediment delivery, connectivity, and reduce egg-to-fry survival



Dynamic covariates (flow, temperature, invasives, lake trout) were held at the selected lag specification throughout. Comparison is by AICc.

text:
Model Fit

Convergence


Show code
conv_label <- ifelse(optB$convergence == 0, "Converged (0)",
                     paste("Not converged (", optB$convergence, ")"))

A convergence code of 0 means the optimizer found a stable solution. The maximum gradient measures how flat the likelihood surface is at the solution; values below 0.01 are considered well-converged.


Observed vs Fitted Redd Counts


Show code
df_obsfit <- fit_plots$fit

ggplot(df_obsfit, aes(x = mu, y = y_obs)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = -Inf, xmax = 10, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = "tomato", alpha = 0.06) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 10, color = "tomato", linetype = 2, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, size = 1.2, color = "grey30") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "steelblue",
              linetype = 2, linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = 10, y = max(df_obsfit$y_obs, na.rm = TRUE) * 0.7,
           label = "N \u2248 20 adults", hjust = -0.1,
           color = "tomato", size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log1p", labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(trans = "log1p", labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(x = "Model-fitted redd count (log scale)",
       y = "Observed redd count (log scale)",
       title = "Observed vs Fitted Redd Counts — All Site-Years",
       subtitle = "Dashed blue line = perfect agreement  |  Red zone = fitted count \u2248 N < 20 adults") +
  theme_bw()









Each point is one site-year observation. Points on the dashed blue line mean the model predicted exactly what was observed. The red shaded zone marks fitted counts below roughly 10 redds — equivalent to approximately 20 adults — the threshold below which populations carry substantial demographic risk of local loss. Per-population time series are shown on the Model Results page.

Residual Diagnostics
The plots below are standard checks that the model is not systematically wrong in any particular direction.


Show code
df_resid <- fit_plots$resid

df_resid |>
  ggplot(aes(x = pearson)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "steelblue", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey40") +
  labs(x = "Pearson Residual", y = "Count",
       title = "Residual Distribution — All Site-Years") +
  theme_bw()









A well-behaved model produces residuals that are roughly symmetric and centered near zero. A long right tail is expected with count data, occasional high redd counts are hard predict.

AR(1) autocorrelation (ρ) — bull trout populations tend to track multi-year environmental cycles, so a good year is often followed by another good year. A positive ρ confirms this persistence is present in the data after covariates are accounted for. Process error (σ_N) — the year-to-year variation in population growth that cannot be explained by any measured covariate; smaller is better but some irreducible stochasticity is always expected. NB2 dispersion (φ_obs) — describes how observed redd counts deviate from expected abundance; lower φ means more variability (see plot below).

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Observation Model: Why NB2?
Redd counts are inherently variable — surveyors count more redds in high-water years, spawning aggregations can be spatially clumped, and observer effort varies across teams and seasons. A simple Poisson model assumes that the variance in counts equals their mean, which turns out to be far too restrictive for these data. The Negative Binomial (NB2) model allows variance to grow faster than the mean, giving it more flexibility to fit the range of counts actually observed without attributing all that variability to real changes in population size.
The plot below provides the evidence: each point is one survey reach (site), showing its long-term mean redd count against its year-to-year variance. The red line is the NB2 prediction; the blue dashed line is what Poisson would predict. The data follow the NB2 curve closely across the full range of abundances — the Poisson line consistently falls below the observed variance, especially at sites with higher redd counts.


Show code
# Build site × year data frame using the already-loaded fit objects
phi_val <- phi_e$est[1]
phi_se_ <- phi_e$se[1]

logN_mat_diag <- matrix(
  fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "logN"],
  nrow = nPop, ncol = data_list$nYears
)

df_diag <- expand.grid(site = seq_len(data_list$nSites),
                       year = seq_len(data_list$nYears)) |>
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(
    pop   = data_list$site_pop[site] + 1L,
    y_obs = as.vector(data_list$ReddCounts),
    mu    = 0.5 * exp(logN_mat_diag[cbind(pop, year)]) * data_list$pS[site]
  ) |>
  filter(is.finite(y_obs), !is.na(y_obs), mu > 0)

# Per-site variance vs mean (at least 5 years of data per site)
site_stats <- df_diag |>
  group_by(site) |>
  summarise(obs_mean = mean(y_obs),
            obs_var  = var(y_obs),
            n        = n(),
            .groups  = "drop") |>
  filter(n >= 5, obs_mean > 0, obs_var > 0)

# Compute global overdispersion statistics for annotation
od_pois <- sum((df_diag$y_obs - df_diag$mu)^2 / df_diag$mu) / nrow(df_diag)
od_nb2  <- sum((df_diag$y_obs - df_diag$mu)^2 /
               (df_diag$mu + df_diag$mu^2 / phi_val)) / nrow(df_diag)

# Theory curves
mu_seq <- 10^seq(log10(min(site_stats$obs_mean)),
                 log10(max(site_stats$obs_mean)),
                 length.out = 300)
var_lines <- tibble(
  mu      = mu_seq,
  Poisson = mu_seq,
  NB2     = mu_seq + mu_seq^2 / phi_val
) |> pivot_longer(-mu, names_to = "model", values_to = "var")

ggplot(site_stats, aes(x = obs_mean, y = obs_var)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 2, colour = "grey30") +
  geom_line(data = var_lines,
            aes(x = mu, y = var, colour = model, linetype = model),
            linewidth = 1.0) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Poisson = "steelblue", NB2 = "tomato"),
                      name = NULL) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c(Poisson = "dashed", NB2 = "solid"),
                        name = NULL) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  annotate("text", x = Inf, y = -Inf, hjust = 1.05, vjust = -0.5, size = 3.2,
           label = sprintf(
             "Poisson disp. = %.1f  |  NB2 disp. = %.2f  |  φ = %.2f (SE %.2f)",
             od_pois, od_nb2, phi_val, phi_se_
           ), colour = "grey30") +
  labs(
    x        = "Site mean redd count (log scale)",
    y        = "Site observed variance (log scale)",
    title    = "Observed variance vs mean — NB2 vs Poisson",
    subtitle = sprintf(
      "NB2: Var = μ + μ²/φ,  φ = %.2f (SE = %.2f);  Poisson: Var = μ",
      phi_val, phi_se_
    )
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")









Points above the Poisson line (blue dashes) represent sites where redd counts are more variable than a Poisson model would predict — which is nearly every site. The NB2 line (red, solid) passes through the cloud of points, confirming that the chosen observation model is appropriate for these data.

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
section: Causal Analysis: Invasives and Bull Trout
text:
Causal Analysis: Invasives and Bull Trout
To determine whether the negative association between non-native trout and bull trout reflects displacement (invasives suppressing bull trout population growth) or replacement (declining bull trout creating space for invasives), we fit cross-lagged mixed-effects regressions using the model-estimated latent log-abundance as the bull trout index. The displacement signal — INV(t−k) predicting log-λ(t) — is large, negative, and highly significant across all lags from 1 to 8 years (|t| = 6–21), with the strongest effect at a 1-year lag and gradual decay at longer horizons as expected from a lagged life-history response. The replacement signal — logN(t) predicting ΔINV(t+k) — is indistinguishable from zero at every lead (|t| < 0.4, R² < 0.01%). The asymmetry between the two directions strongly supports a causal pathway from invasive trout to bull trout suppression, rather than the reverse.


Show code
cl <- readRDS(here("Results", "CrossLag_INV_BLT_2026-03-23.RDS"))

inv2blt <- cl$results_inv2blt |>
  mutate(sig = abs(t_stat) > 2,
         panel = "INV(t\u2212k) \u2192 log\u03bb(t)   [Displacement: invasives drive bull trout decline]")

blt2inv <- cl$results_blt2inv |>
  mutate(sig = abs(t_stat) > 2,
         panel = "logN(t) \u2192 \u0394INV(t+k)   [Replacement: bull trout decline drives invasion]")

bind_rows(inv2blt, blt2inv) |>
  mutate(panel = factor(panel, levels = c(
    "INV(t\u2212k) \u2192 log\u03bb(t)   [Displacement: invasives drive bull trout decline]",
    "logN(t) \u2192 \u0394INV(t+k)   [Replacement: bull trout decline drives invasion]"
  ))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = lag_yr, y = beta)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower95, ymax = upper95, fill = sig),
              alpha = 0.18) +
  geom_line(aes(color = sig), linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(color = sig, fill = sig),
             shape = 21, size = 3, stroke = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = "grey60",  `TRUE` = "steelblue"),
                     labels = c(`FALSE` = "|t| \u2264 2", `TRUE` = "|t| > 2"),
                     name = NULL) +
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c(`FALSE` = "grey60",  `TRUE` = "steelblue"),
                    labels  = c(`FALSE` = "|t| \u2264 2", `TRUE` = "|t| > 2"),
                    name    = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:8) +
  facet_wrap(~panel, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    x       = "Lag / lead (years)",
    y       = "Regression coefficient (± 95% CI)",
    title   = "Cross-lagged analysis: causal direction between invasives and bull trout",
    caption = paste0("Mixed-effects regression on model-estimated latent log-abundance.  ",
                     "Run date: ", cl$run_date)
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    strip.text       = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









The top panel asks: does a higher non-native fish proportion in past years predict lower bull trout growth today? The answer is a clear yes — the effect is large and negative at every lag tested, and the confidence band (shaded ribbon) stays well below zero. The bottom panel asks the reverse: does a decline in bull trout abundance predict an increase in non-natives in future years? The answer is no — the line never departs meaningfully from zero. This asymmetry is the key evidence that invasives are suppressing bull trout, not simply filling space left behind by a declining population.

Species-specific displacement lags
Both brook trout (BKT) and brown trout (BNT) independently displace bull trout, but at different time scales: BKT effects peak at lags 1–2 years, consistent with direct headwater competition among juveniles, while BNT effects are strongest at lags 3–5 years, suggesting a slower pathway possibly mediated through displacement of juvenile bull trout cohorts before they recruit to the adult stage. Neither species shows any evidence of a replacement signal — bull trout decline does not predict subsequent increases in either invader, ruling out a vacancy-driven mechanism and reinforcing the causal direction from invasive trout to bull trout suppression.


Show code
cls <- readRDS(here("Results", "CrossLag_INV_BLT_Species_2026-03-23.RDS"))

species_pal <- c(
  "Brook trout (BKT)" = "steelblue",
  "Brown trout (BNT)" = "tomato",
  "Combined"          = "grey40"
)

cls$results_all |>
  filter(species != "Combined") |>
  mutate(
    sig   = abs(t_stat) > 2,
    panel = case_when(
      direction == "INV \u2192 BLT" ~
        "INV(t\u2212k) \u2192 log\u03bb(t)   [Displacement]",
      direction == "BLT \u2192 INV" ~
        "logN(t) \u2192 \u0394INV(t+k)   [Replacement]"
    ),
    panel = factor(panel, levels = c(
      "INV(t\u2212k) \u2192 log\u03bb(t)   [Displacement]",
      "logN(t) \u2192 \u0394INV(t+k)   [Replacement]"
    ))
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = lag_yr, y = beta,
             color = species, fill = species, shape = sig)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower95, ymax = upper95), alpha = 0.12,
              color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_point(size = 3, stroke = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = species_pal, name = NULL) +
  scale_fill_manual(values  = species_pal, name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = 21, `TRUE` = 19),
                     labels = c(`FALSE` = "|t| \u2264 2", `TRUE` = "|t| > 2"),
                     name   = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:8) +
  facet_wrap(~panel, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    x       = "Lag / lead (years)",
    y       = "Regression coefficient (\u00b1 95% CI)",
    title   = "Species-specific cross-lagged analysis: BKT and BNT",
    caption = paste0("Filled points: |t| > 2.  Run date: ", cls$run_date)
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    strip.text       = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









Brook trout (blue) and brown trout (red) both suppress bull trout, but the timing differs. Brook trout effects are strongest at short lags (1–2 years), consistent with direct competition for space and food in headwater streams where both species co-occur as juveniles. Brown trout effects peak later (3–5 years), suggesting a slower pathway — possibly displacing juvenile bull trout cohorts that would otherwise have survived to spawn several years later. Neither species shows any replacement signal in the bottom panel.

title: Conservation Prioritization
text:
This page presents a demographic status assessment for all 80 local bull trout populations across 26 core areas in the Columbia Headwaters Recovery Unit. All estimates are derived from a hierarchical state-space Ricker model fitted to annual redd count time series (Model v1_22 / Data v1_10).
The assessment addresses two management questions:

Where are populations headed? — The intrinsic growth rate (λ) estimated under recent covariate conditions reveals which populations are growing, stable, or declining.
Which threats matter most, and for which populations? — Sensitivity scenarios quantify how much λ would improve if a single threat lever were reduced while all others remain at current levels, highlighting where management investment would have the greatest return.

This page presents a demographic status assessment for all 80 local bull trout populations across 26 core areas in the Columbia Headwaters Recovery Unit. All estimates are derived from a hierarchical state-space Ricker model fitted to annual redd count time series (Model v1_22 / Data v1_10).
The assessment addresses two management questions:

Where are populations headed? — The intrinsic growth rate (λ) estimated under recent covariate conditions reveals which populations are growing, stable, or declining.
Which threats matter most, and for which populations? — Sensitivity scenarios quantify how much λ would improve if a single threat lever were reduced while all others remain at current levels, highlighting where management investment would have the greatest return.

title: Conservation Prioritization
text:
Key Findings






Population-Level Summary




64 of 80 populations (80%) have λ < 1 under recent conditions, indicating ongoing decline.
λ ranges from 0.81 (most rapidly declining) to 1.12 (most rapidly growing) across all populations.
55 populations have ≥ 50% probability of dropping below 20 adults at some point in the next 7 years (one bull trout generation).
Every population carries some demographic risk over the projection horizon — underscoring the recovery unit-wide urgency of threat management.

title: Conservation Prioritization
text:
Population Status Table
The table below is sortable, filterable, and searchable. Use the Core Area column to focus on specific drainages, or sort any column to identify priority populations.
Column guide:







Column group
What it shows




Current Status
Median λ with 90% credible interval under last-observed covariate conditions. λ > 1 = growing; λ < 1 = declining.


Demographic Risk
Probability of the latent adult population falling below a threshold at any point in a 7-year forward projection (mean environmental conditions).


Sensitivity
Median λ if a single threat lever is improved to the level shown, with all other covariates held at current. These highlight the biggest single-lever opportunity per population.










Table sorted by ascending λ by default (most declining populations first).






Sensitivity column definitions












Column
Scenario




INV → avg
Invasive fish proportion reset to the dataset-wide historical mean (z = 0)


INV → min
Invasive fish proportion at the lowest historically observed value — best-case eradication scenario


Temp − 1SD
August stream temperature reduced by one historical standard deviation — simulates riparian or climate-relief benefit


Flow + 1SD
Summer base-flow increased by one historical standard deviation — simulates flow augmentation


LKT → min
Lake trout proportion at the historical minimum for lake-connected cores; “—” for non-lake populations



All sensitivity scenarios hold all other covariates at their most recent observed value.



Model v1_22 · Data v1_10 · Run date: 2026-03-25 · 1000 marginal posterior draws

title: Conservation Prioritization
text:
Scenario Explorer
The interactive scenario tool extends the sensitivity analysis by letting you set any combination of covariate conditions simultaneously — useful for evaluating management packages that address multiple threats at once, and for identifying which populations respond most strongly to each lever.

  
    ▶  Launch Scenario Explorer
  
  
    Opens in a new tab  ·  powered by shinyapps.io
  

The tool provides three views:

λ Comparison — bar chart of median λ per population under current vs. scenario conditions, coloured by core area
Risk Biplot — scatter plot of intrinsic growth rate (b₀ᵣ) vs. probability of local population loss; bubble size reflects habitat extent; a useful prioritization framework combining population trajectory and demographic vulnerability
Data Table — the full status table with scenario-adjusted values, downloadable as CSV

title: Conservation Prioritization
text:
Methods Note
Demographic status is estimated from the hierarchical Ricker state-space model described on the Model Results page. Briefly:

Process model — log-abundance follows an AR(1) Ricker equation with population-level random slopes for flow, summer temperature, invasive fish proportion, and lake trout proportion (lake-connected cores only).
Observation model — annual redd counts are NB2-distributed given latent abundance, scaled by survey-section detection and a contemporaneous flow effect on observer efficiency.
λ estimation — intrinsic growth rate at each population is computed from 1,000 marginal posterior draws (independent Normal approximations centred on MAP estimates), providing the median and 90% credible interval shown in the table.
Demographic risk projection — 7-year forward simulations using MAP state estimates as initial conditions and AR(1) process noise drawn from the posterior. No management intervention is assumed. Results are reported as the probability that latent adult abundance falls below a threshold at any point during the projection horizon.

title: Data Overview
text:
This page documents the data assembled across 80 local populations in 26 core areas, spanning 1984–2022. All covariates were compiled from multiple state, federal, and academic sources and processed into a common format for the hierarchical model. The sections below describe each data type, its source, and the transformations applied before model fitting.



Annual redd counts are the primary abundance index. Spawning redds (nests) are counted by technicians walking fixed survey reaches each fall; counts are proportional to the number of spawning adults and have been used to track bull trout population trends across the recovery units for more than two decades.
Source: USGS-harmonized redd count database compiled from USFWS, Montana FWP, Idaho IDFG, and tribal monitoring programs.
Coverage: Survey sites with at least 10 years of data are retained. Within each population, multiple survey reaches (sites) contribute counts in the same year; the population-level total is the sum across sites. A count of zero is recorded when a site was surveyed but no redds were observed; years when a site was not surveyed are treated as missing (not zero).
In the model: Redd counts enter as the observed response. Modeled as NB2 (negative binomial, fixed dispersion) conditioned on latent adult abundance, survey-reach length, and a contemporaneous flow effect on detection probability.




The following covariates vary by population and year and enter the population process equation with a 6-year lag (5 years specified in data prep; the C++ model adds 1 additional year). This lag reflects the approximate time from egg incubation under a given year’s environmental conditions to first-time spawning as an adult — roughly one bull trout generation.





Covariate
Spatial scale
Metric
Transformation
Source




Summer streamflow
Population
Annual sum of daily discharge
Log → z-score → 6-yr lag
USGS stream gauge network; ProcessedFlowMetrics.csv


August temperature
Population
Mean August stream temperature (°C)
Z-score → 6-yr lag
NorWeST spatially-continuous stream temperature model (Isaak et al. 2016); MeanPatchAnnualTemps.RDS


Non-native fish proportion (INV)
Population
Proportion of non-native salmonids (brook + brown trout) in electrofishing catch
Floor at 0.1% → logit → z-score; forward-filled for missing years
Electrofishing surveys compiled from MFWP, USFWS, Tribes; BLTPatch_InvasiveRatio


Lake trout index (LKT)
Core area
Proportion of lake trout in netting catch
Logit → z-score; forward-filled for missing years
Annual netting surveys — Glacier NP and MFWP; LakeNetting/







A contemporaneous flow term (same year, no lag) is also included in the observation model to account for year-to-year variation in redd detectability — high-flow years reduce observer efficiency and can suppress counts independently of true abundance changes.
Lake trout data are measured at the core area level (not population) and are only available for lake-connected systems. A binary indicator flags which core areas have LKT data; the LKT effect is estimated only for those cores.




The following covariates are fixed through time and characterize the physical setting and life-history context of each population. They inform the population-level mean growth rate (b₀ᵣ) through a regression on static population attributes.





Covariate
Metric
Transformation
Source




Valley bottom extent (VB)
Riparian valley floor area (km²)
Log(x + 1) → z-score
GIS — Whited, Flathead Lake Biological Station


W95 flood frequency
Mean annual days exceeding 95th-pct flow
Log(x + 1) → z-score
Derived from USGS stream gauge flow records


Road density — valley bottom (RDVB)
Road length per km² of valley bottom
Log(x + 1) → z-score
National road layer intersected with valley bottom GIS — Whited, FLBS


Adfluvial life history
Binary: population has lake-resident life-history access
0/1
Core area life-history classification — USFWS Recovery Plan; CoreArea_LifeHistory.csv


Habitat extent (km)
Connected spawning habitat length (km)
Used as density denominator in process model
GIS — connected habitat layer, BLTPatches_metrics







Valley bottom extent and road density were derived from GIS layers developed by Diane Whited at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. These capture landscape-scale habitat availability and disturbance pressure that can affect spawning habitat quality and population productivity independently of year-to-year environmental variation.




The plots below show, for each local population: annual redd counts (bars = totals, points = individual survey-site counts), summer streamflow, August temperature, and non-native trout proportion. Core area sections also include a lake trout abundance index (where available) and a static covariate summary table. Covariate time series are displayed in their native units; the model uses z-scored and lagged versions.

title: Data Overview
text:
This page documents the data assembled across 80 local populations in 26 core areas, spanning 1984–2022. All covariates were compiled from multiple state, federal, and academic sources and processed into a common format for the hierarchical model. The sections below describe each data type, its source, and the transformations applied before model fitting.



Annual redd counts are the primary abundance index. Spawning redds (nests) are counted by technicians walking fixed survey reaches each fall; counts are proportional to the number of spawning adults and have been used to track bull trout population trends across the recovery units for more than two decades.
Source: USGS-harmonized redd count database compiled from USFWS, Montana FWP, Idaho IDFG, and tribal monitoring programs.
Coverage: Survey sites with at least 10 years of data are retained. Within each population, multiple survey reaches (sites) contribute counts in the same year; the population-level total is the sum across sites. A count of zero is recorded when a site was surveyed but no redds were observed; years when a site was not surveyed are treated as missing (not zero).
In the model: Redd counts enter as the observed response. Modeled as NB2 (negative binomial, fixed dispersion) conditioned on latent adult abundance, survey-reach length, and a contemporaneous flow effect on detection probability.




The following covariates vary by population and year and enter the population process equation with a 6-year lag (5 years specified in data prep; the C++ model adds 1 additional year). This lag reflects the approximate time from egg incubation under a given year’s environmental conditions to first-time spawning as an adult — roughly one bull trout generation.





Covariate
Spatial scale
Metric
Transformation
Source




Summer streamflow
Population
Annual sum of daily discharge
Log → z-score → 6-yr lag
USGS stream gauge network; ProcessedFlowMetrics.csv


August temperature
Population
Mean August stream temperature (°C)
Z-score → 6-yr lag
NorWeST spatially-continuous stream temperature model (Isaak et al. 2016); MeanPatchAnnualTemps.RDS


Non-native fish proportion (INV)
Population
Proportion of non-native salmonids (brook + brown trout) in electrofishing catch
Floor at 0.1% → logit → z-score; forward-filled for missing years
Electrofishing surveys compiled from MFWP, USFWS, Tribes; BLTPatch_InvasiveRatio


Lake trout index (LKT)
Core area
Proportion of lake trout in netting catch
Logit → z-score; forward-filled for missing years
Annual netting surveys — Glacier NP and MFWP; LakeNetting/







A contemporaneous flow term (same year, no lag) is also included in the observation model to account for year-to-year variation in redd detectability — high-flow years reduce observer efficiency and can suppress counts independently of true abundance changes.
Lake trout data are measured at the core area level (not population) and are only available for lake-connected systems. A binary indicator flags which core areas have LKT data; the LKT effect is estimated only for those cores.




The following covariates are fixed through time and characterize the physical setting and life-history context of each population. They inform the population-level mean growth rate (b₀ᵣ) through a regression on static population attributes.





Covariate
Metric
Transformation
Source




Valley bottom extent (VB)
Riparian valley floor area (km²)
Log(x + 1) → z-score
GIS — Whited, Flathead Lake Biological Station


W95 flood frequency
Mean annual days exceeding 95th-pct flow
Log(x + 1) → z-score
Derived from USGS stream gauge flow records


Road density — valley bottom (RDVB)
Road length per km² of valley bottom
Log(x + 1) → z-score
National road layer intersected with valley bottom GIS — Whited, FLBS


Adfluvial life history
Binary: population has lake-resident life-history access
0/1
Core area life-history classification — USFWS Recovery Plan; CoreArea_LifeHistory.csv


Habitat extent (km)
Connected spawning habitat length (km)
Used as density denominator in process model
GIS — connected habitat layer, BLTPatches_metrics







Valley bottom extent and road density were derived from GIS layers developed by Diane Whited at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. These capture landscape-scale habitat availability and disturbance pressure that can affect spawning habitat quality and population productivity independently of year-to-year environmental variation.




The plots below show, for each local population: annual redd counts (bars = totals, points = individual survey-site counts), summer streamflow, August temperature, and non-native trout proportion. Core area sections also include a lake trout abundance index (where available) and a static covariate summary table. Covariate time series are displayed in their native units; the model uses z-scored and lagged versions.

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Observation Model
Redd counts are inherently variable. Environme surveyors count more redds in high-water years, spawning aggregations can be spatially clumped, and observer effort varies across teams and seasons. A simple Poisson model assumes that the variance in counts equals their mean, which turns out to be far too restrictive for these data. The Negative Binomial (NB2) model allows variance to grow faster than the mean, giving it more flexibility to fit the range of counts actually observed without attributing all that variability to changes in population size.
In the plot below, each point is one survey reach (site), showing its long-term mean redd count against its year-to-year variance. The red line is the NB2 prediction; the blue dashed line is what Poisson would predict. The data follow the NB2 curve closely across the full range of abundances and the Poisson line consistently falls below the observed variance, especially at sites with higher redd counts.


Show code
# Build site × year data frame using the already-loaded fit objects
phi_val <- phi_e$est[1]
phi_se_ <- phi_e$se[1]

logN_mat_diag <- matrix(
  fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "logN"],
  nrow = nPop, ncol = data_list$nYears
)

df_diag <- expand.grid(site = seq_len(data_list$nSites),
                       year = seq_len(data_list$nYears)) |>
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(
    pop   = data_list$site_pop[site] + 1L,
    y_obs = as.vector(data_list$ReddCounts),
    mu    = 0.5 * exp(logN_mat_diag[cbind(pop, year)]) * data_list$pS[site]
  ) |>
  filter(is.finite(y_obs), !is.na(y_obs), mu > 0)

# Per-site variance vs mean (at least 5 years of data per site)
site_stats <- df_diag |>
  group_by(site) |>
  summarise(obs_mean = mean(y_obs),
            obs_var  = var(y_obs),
            n        = n(),
            .groups  = "drop") |>
  filter(n >= 5, obs_mean > 0, obs_var > 0)

# Compute global overdispersion statistics for annotation
od_pois <- sum((df_diag$y_obs - df_diag$mu)^2 / df_diag$mu) / nrow(df_diag)
od_nb2  <- sum((df_diag$y_obs - df_diag$mu)^2 /
               (df_diag$mu + df_diag$mu^2 / phi_val)) / nrow(df_diag)

# Theory curves
mu_seq <- 10^seq(log10(min(site_stats$obs_mean)),
                 log10(max(site_stats$obs_mean)),
                 length.out = 300)
var_lines <- tibble(
  mu      = mu_seq,
  Poisson = mu_seq,
  NB2     = mu_seq + mu_seq^2 / phi_val
) |> pivot_longer(-mu, names_to = "model", values_to = "var")

ggplot(site_stats, aes(x = obs_mean, y = obs_var)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 2, colour = "grey30") +
  geom_line(data = var_lines,
            aes(x = mu, y = var, colour = model, linetype = model),
            linewidth = 1.0) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Poisson = "steelblue", NB2 = "tomato"),
                      name = NULL) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c(Poisson = "dashed", NB2 = "solid"),
                        name = NULL) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  annotate("text", x = Inf, y = -Inf, hjust = 1.05, vjust = -0.5, size = 3.2,
           label = sprintf(
             "Poisson disp. = %.1f  |  NB2 disp. = %.2f  |  φ = %.2f (SE %.2f)",
             od_pois, od_nb2, phi_val, phi_se_
           ), colour = "grey30") +
  labs(
    x        = "Site mean redd count (log scale)",
    y        = "Site observed variance (log scale)",
    title    = "Observed variance vs mean — NB2 vs Poisson",
    subtitle = sprintf(
      "NB2: Var = μ + μ²/φ,  φ = %.2f (SE = %.2f);  Poisson: Var = μ",
      phi_val, phi_se_
    )
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")









Points above the Poisson line (blue dashes) represent sites where redd counts are more variable than a Poisson model would predict — which is nearly every site. The NB2 line (red, solid) passes through the cloud of points, confirming that the chosen observation model is appropriate for these data.

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
section: Grainger Causality Analysis: Invasives effect on Bull Trout
text:
Grainger Causality Analysis: Invasives effect on Bull Trout
When using dynamic invasive species data (time series), a negative association between non-native trout and bull trout could reflect displacement (invasives negatively affecting bull trout population growth) or replacement (declining bull trout creating space for invasives). Displacement would be needed for management-based suppression or control efforts to improve bull trout population status. To test these two hypotheses, we performed a grainger causality analysis by fitting cross-lagged mixed-effects regressions using the model-estimated latent log-abundance as the bull trout index. If INV are displacing bull trout through competitive interctions then increases in invasive abudance would lead to decreases in bull trout population growth rate. If replacement were occuring then declines in bull trout abundance would precede increases in INV abudance. The displacement signal [ INV(t−k) predicting log-λ(t) ] is large, negative, and highly significant across all lags from 1 to 8 years (|t| = 6–21), with the strongest effect at a 1-year lag and gradual decay at longer horizons as expected from a lagged life-history response. The replacement signal [ logN(t) predicting ΔINV(t+k) ] is indistinguishable from zero at every lead (|t| < 0.4, R² < 0.01%). The asymmetry between the two directions strongly supports a causal pathway from invasive trout to bull trout suppression, rather than the reverse.


Show code
cl <- readRDS(here("Results", "CrossLag_INV_BLT_2026-03-23.RDS"))

inv2blt <- cl$results_inv2blt |>
  mutate(sig = abs(t_stat) > 2,
         panel = "INV(t\u2212k) \u2192 log\u03bb(t)   [Displacement: invasives drive bull trout decline]")

blt2inv <- cl$results_blt2inv |>
  mutate(sig = abs(t_stat) > 2,
         panel = "logN(t) \u2192 \u0394INV(t+k)   [Replacement: bull trout decline drives invasion]")

bind_rows(inv2blt, blt2inv) |>
  mutate(panel = factor(panel, levels = c(
    "INV(t\u2212k) \u2192 log\u03bb(t)   [Displacement: invasives drive bull trout decline]",
    "logN(t) \u2192 \u0394INV(t+k)   [Replacement: bull trout decline drives invasion]"
  ))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = lag_yr, y = beta)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower95, ymax = upper95, fill = sig),
              alpha = 0.18) +
  geom_line(aes(color = sig), linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(color = sig, fill = sig),
             shape = 21, size = 3, stroke = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = "grey60",  `TRUE` = "steelblue"),
                     labels = c(`FALSE` = "|t| \u2264 2", `TRUE` = "|t| > 2"),
                     name = NULL) +
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c(`FALSE` = "grey60",  `TRUE` = "steelblue"),
                    labels  = c(`FALSE` = "|t| \u2264 2", `TRUE` = "|t| > 2"),
                    name    = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:8) +
  facet_wrap(~panel, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    x       = "Lag / lead (years)",
    y       = "Regression coefficient (± 95% CI)",
    title   = "Cross-lagged analysis: causal direction between invasives and bull trout",
    caption = paste0("Mixed-effects regression on model-estimated latent log-abundance.  ",
                     "Run date: ", cl$run_date)
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    strip.text       = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )










Species-specific displacement lags
Both brook trout (BKT) and brown trout (BNT) independently potentially displace bull trout, but at different time scales. This plot decomposes the invasive effect observed in the model into marginal effects of each species. Both show strong evidence for a displacement signal. Neither species shows any evidence of a replacement signal.


Show code
cls <- readRDS(here("Results", "CrossLag_INV_BLT_Species_2026-03-23.RDS"))

species_pal <- c(
  "Brook trout (BKT)" = "steelblue",
  "Brown trout (BNT)" = "tomato",
  "Combined"          = "grey40"
)

cls$results_all |>
  filter(species != "Combined") |>
  mutate(
    sig   = abs(t_stat) > 2,
    panel = case_when(
      direction == "INV \u2192 BLT" ~
        "INV(t\u2212k) \u2192 log\u03bb(t)   [Displacement]",
      direction == "BLT \u2192 INV" ~
        "logN(t) \u2192 \u0394INV(t+k)   [Replacement]"
    ),
    panel = factor(panel, levels = c(
      "INV(t\u2212k) \u2192 log\u03bb(t)   [Displacement]",
      "logN(t) \u2192 \u0394INV(t+k)   [Replacement]"
    ))
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = lag_yr, y = beta,
             color = species, fill = species, shape = sig)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower95, ymax = upper95), alpha = 0.12,
              color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_point(size = 3, stroke = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = species_pal, name = NULL) +
  scale_fill_manual(values  = species_pal, name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = 21, `TRUE` = 19),
                     labels = c(`FALSE` = "|t| \u2264 2", `TRUE` = "|t| > 2"),
                     name   = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:8) +
  facet_wrap(~panel, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    x       = "Lag / lead (years)",
    y       = "Regression coefficient (\u00b1 95% CI)",
    title   = "Species-specific cross-lagged analysis: BKT and BNT",
    caption = paste0("Filled points: |t| > 2.  Run date: ", cls$run_date)
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    strip.text       = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
section: Grainger causality analysis: invasive effects on Bull Trout
text:
Grainger causality analysis: invasive effects on Bull Trout
When analyzing time series of invasive and bull trout populations, negative associations can arise from two distinct processes. First, displacement, where increases in invasive trout reduce bull trout abundance. Second, replacement, where declines in bull trout create ecological opportunity for invasive species to increase. Distinguishing these mechanisms is essential for evaluating whether suppression of invasive species can improve bull trout population status.
To differentiate these alternatives, we conducted a Granger causality analysis using cross-lagged mixed-effects regressions based on model-estimated latent log-abundance. Under a displacement hypothesis, increases in invasive abundance at time t − k should predict subsequent declines in bull trout abundance. Conversely, under a replacement hypothesis, declines in bull trout abundance should precede increases in invasive abundance.
We found strong temporal asymmetry between these pathways. Increases in invasive trout abundance consistently predicted subsequent declines in bull trout abundance (see below figure), providing strong support for displacement. Evidence for replacement is weak at all time lags (|t| < 0.4, R² < 0.01%). Although Granger causality does not establish mechanistic causation, this asymmetry is strong evidence of a directional effect.

  mutate(sig = abs(t_stat) > 2,

  mutate(sig = abs(t_stat) > 2,

title: Core Area Status
text:
Core areas are the primary unit of viability assessment for bull trout in the Columbia Headwaters Recovery Unit. Each core area encompasses one or more local spawning populations that share probable connectivity through adult dispersal and juvenile drift, approximating a probable metapopulation. In practice, core area boundaries also reflect watershed structure, jurisdictional units (state, tribal, national forest), and the spatial extent of long-term monitoring programmes. They are a pragmatic but biologically grounded scale for conservation planning — neither so fine as individual spawning reaches nor so coarse as the full recovery unit.
This analysis covers 26 core areas containing 80 monitored local populations, with redd-count time series spanning 1984–2022. Abundance and growth-rate estimates are derived from the hierarchical state-space Ricker model (Model v1_22 / Data v1_8).

title: Core Area Status
text:
Measuring Core Area Status
A core area can be evaluated from several distinct perspectives. The metrics below are not interchangeable: a core area may score well on one and poorly on another, and the most relevant metric depends on the management objective.









Metric
What it measures
Best suited to…
Key limitation




Abundance (N/km)
Total estimated fish per km of connected spawning habitat
Tracking overall recovery numbers
Insensitive to how fish are distributed across the spatial network


Growth rate (λ)
Intrinsic per-capita growth rate at the core level
Identifying recovering vs. declining units
Backward-looking; does not directly capture future persistence


Occupancy
Proportion of local populations currently above a minimum viable size (≥ 20 adults)
Detecting network degradation and local extirpations
Satisfied even if all occupied populations are very small


Portfolio balance
Effective number of populations relative to total, derived from the Shannon diversity of the abundance distribution
Detecting over-concentration of fish in a single dominant population
High balance does not imply high total abundance


Persistence (1 − Qext)
Probability the core avoids quasi-extinction over a 7-year forward projection
Long-term viability assessment under current trajectory
Sensitive to model assumptions and projection horizon




Why occupancy and portfolio balance matter
A core area that has lost populations has lost structural redundancy. In metapopulation theory, the spatial network of local populations provides a buffer against stochastic fluctuations: a bad year in one tributary can be rescued by dispersal from neighbouring populations. When local extirpations reduce the number of occupied patches, the remaining populations must carry the full weight of recolonisation and demographic rescue — a structurally degraded network even if total fish counts remain high.
Portfolio balance captures a subtler version of the same concern. If 90% of a core area’s fish inhabit a single valley, that core is functionally closer to a single population than to a true metapopulation. A wildfire, disease pulse, or drought affecting the dominant population could effectively collapse the core even though four other populations nominally persist. The effective number of populations — exp(Shannon entropy of the within-core abundance distribution) — measures this: a core with five perfectly equal populations scores 5; a core dominated by one population scores close to 1, regardless of total abundance.


Composite scoring
Aggregating these metrics into a single score requires value judgements about weights and commensurability. Rather than imposing a fixed composite, the heatmap below presents all five metrics simultaneously, with each metric standardised relative to the range across all core areas. This allows managers to identify which dimensions of core area status are most critical for a given core, and to apply the weighting most consistent with their conservation objectives.

title: Core Area Status
text: Multi-Metric Status Overview

title: Core Area Status
text:
Composite Metrics: Multiple Perspectives
No single metric captures core area status fully. The five metrics used here each prioritise a different conservation objective and are not interchangeable.








Metric
Conservation objective
Key limitation




Abundance (N/km)
Maximise total fish
Blind to spatial distribution


Growth rate (λ)
Identify recovering vs. declining units
Backward-looking


Occupancy
Maintain all extant local populations (≥ 20 adults)
Insensitive to population size


Portfolio balance
Detect over-concentration in a single dominant population
Independent of total abundance


Persistence (1 − Qext)
Long-term viability under current trajectory
Projection-dependent



Occupancy and portfolio balance deserve particular attention. A core that has lost populations has reduced network redundancy regardless of total abundance — remaining populations carry the full burden of demographic rescue. Portfolio balance (effective populations / total, from Shannon diversity of the abundance distribution) flags cores where fish are concentrated in one tributary: functionally a single population even if others nominally persist.
The heatmap below presents all five metrics simultaneously, each standardised to the range across core areas, so managers can apply their own weighting rather than a fixed composite.
